question_text:
A winery in the EU has a large volume of unsold still wine after a difficult season. options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'Assume emergency distillation is intended as a stable permanent channel for surplus wine, and apply the same labeling rules to both bottlings because variety and harvest date are handled identically across the two categories.'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Treat crisis distillation as a measure the EU aims to reduce over time, and review the two bottlings separately because production and label rules differ by category, including how year and grape name can appear.'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Use one common labeling template for both legal categories, but avoid distillation because the EU only limits cellar practices and does not express a policy goal about surplus-disposal interventions.'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Keep the two bottlings under separate winemaking rules, but expect no restrictions on mentioning grape or vintage on labels once the wine enters a distillation scheme.'}]
source_facts:
[1] The labelling regulations include requirements for how grape varieties and vintage may be mentioned on the label.
---
[2] European Union wine regulations has been stated several times since then that the ambition is to phase out interventions such as emergency distillation, since they are "artificial outlets" for wine.

question_text: A winemaker is planning a new Vitis vinifera block in a site where a root-feeding, aphid-like pest is already present. Which plan best fits these conditions?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'Use grafted V. vinifera planting material across the block, while also accounting for cultivar-by-cultivar differences in vulnerability when choosing what to plant.'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Plant all V. vinifera on their own roots because the insect mainly attacks above-ground tissues rather than the root system.'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Skip grafting and rely only on choosing less vulnerable cultivars, since varietal differences remove the need for root protection.'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Avoid grafting only because the pest comes from the eastern United States, so it is not a meaningful risk outside its native area.'}]
source_facts:

---
[2] V. vinifera requires grafting to combat its susceptibility to the root louse Phylloxera.
---
[3] Grape phylloxera is an aphid-like insect that feeds on grape roots and is native to the eastern United States.
